name: Planet Labs PBC

profile.description: Planet Labs PBC designs, constructs, and operates the world's largest fleet of Earth-imaging satellites, capturing daily imagery at up to 3.5-meter resolution (SuperDove) and 50-centimeter resolution (SkySat/Pelican). The company also operates Tanager hyperspectral satellites and delivers geospatial data and analytics through its Earth Observation platform via APIs and browser-based tools. Structured as a Public Benefit Corporation, Planet serves agriculture, energy, forestry, finance, insurance, and government customers. Founded in 2010, headquartered in San Francisco.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Planet launched the Pelican next-generation satellite constellation in 2025 and the Tanager hyperspectral imaging satellite for the Carbon Mapper coalition, representing meaningful new product categories beyond its core SuperDove fleet [1][2]. Revenue grew 26% YoY to $307.7M in FY2026, with Defense & Intelligence surging over 50%, demonstrating successful market expansion. AI Deployment (1/2): Planet has repositioned itself as a 'Space and AI' company, deploying AI algorithms that detect new roads in rainforests — reducing deforestation rates by 55% in Brazil according to CEO Will Marshall [2]. AI-driven analytics are integrated into the platform, though AI-specific revenue is not separately quantified. Market Share (1/2): Planet operates the largest commercial Earth-imaging constellation globally with 200+ satellites, but faces competition from Maxar, BlackSky, and Capella Space; not a dominant market share leader in the broader geospatial analytics market. Product Pipeline (1/2): Pelican constellation (sub-50cm resolution) is expanding, and Tanager-2 hyperspectral missions are in development, though specific revenue targets for new products are not disclosed. R&D Intensity (1/1): R&D spending is significant relative to revenue for a sub-$500M company, reflecting the capital-intensive nature of satellite development and AI analytics.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Planet's mission — 'to use space to help life on Earth' — is specific, thoughtful, and distinctly connected to environmental monitoring and global transparency. As a Public Benefit Corporation, the company has legally embedded its purpose into its governance structure, and CEO Will Marshall (former NASA scientist) consistently references this mission in strategic decisions [2]. The mission shapes product priorities (deforestation detection, methane monitoring) and differentiates from defense-first competitors. Community Impact (1/2): Planet's data has been used to support cultural heritage protection in Ukraine with the Global Heritage Fund and environmental monitoring in the Amazon, representing meaningful mission-aligned community investment, though no specific dollar amounts for philanthropic programs are publicly reported [3]. Employee Retention (0/2): With 945 employees, Planet's executive team approval score ranks in the bottom 40% for companies of its size according to Comparably, and no specific retention or tenure data is publicly available [2]. Insider Buying (1/2): Co-founder and CEO Will Marshall maintains significant equity ownership and his compensation is heavily stock-based, though no notable net insider buying in the last 12 months was identified.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. Planet delivered $307.7M in FY2026 revenue (up 26% YoY) with strong top-line momentum, and achieved its first full year of positive adjusted EBITDA at $15.5M [1]. However, the company remains deeply unprofitable on a GAAP basis with -80.2% profit margin, -78.4% ROE, and a net loss of $59.2M in Q3 alone. D/E of 245% is elevated against $462M total debt, though partially offset by $640M in cash. Forward P/E is negative (unprofitable), preventing PEG calculation. The 3-year revenue CAGR of 17.2% and 41.1% YoY growth are strong positives. Free cash flow turned positive at $234M TTM, a significant milestone for the business model. Liquidity is adequate at $545M daily turnover.

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (6/9): Planet's core business — designing, launching, and operating a fleet of 200+ Earth-imaging satellites — requires physical space infrastructure, orbital mechanics expertise, and regulatory licenses that AI cannot replicate or substitute [1][2]. The proprietary daily global imagery dataset spanning over a decade is a unique, irreplaceable asset. However, AI-powered analytics companies could theoretically source imagery from competitors or public satellites, reducing Planet's pricing power on the data layer. The analytics platform faces competition from AI-native geospatial startups. The hardware moat is strong, but the software/analytics margin is more exposed. AI Opportunity (1/1): Planet is actively deploying AI as a core product capability — CEO Marshall describes the company as a 'Space and AI' business, with AI algorithms processing satellite imagery for deforestation detection, methane monitoring, and agricultural analytics [2]. AI integration into the platform drives customer value and retention, though AI-specific revenue is not separately disclosed.

infinite_game.summary: Planet Labs under founder-CEO Will Marshall exhibits genuine infinite game thinking. The company's Public Benefit Corporation structure legally enshrines its mission beyond shareholder returns, and the 'use space to help life on Earth' purpose has been consistent since founding in 2010. However, the company is still pre-profitability after 16 years, raising questions about whether the cause can sustain itself without proving financial durability. The willingness to invest in long-cycle satellite development and hyperspectral missions reflects infinite thinking, but the company hasn't yet faced the kind of crisis that tests whether leadership holds to the cause when it's costly.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Planet's mission to 'use space to help life on Earth' is genuine, distinctive, and validated by consistent execution — from building the world's largest Earth-imaging fleet to deploying deforestation detection AI in the Amazon and supporting heritage protection in Ukraine [2][3]. The PBC structure legally embeds purpose into governance. However, the company has only operated for 16 years and hasn't yet survived a major crisis or leadership transition that would validate the cause at the highest level.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Planet's 945-person team operates in a mission-driven environment with recently added senior leaders (Kevin Kirn as CPTO, Katie Penn as SVP Marketing) signaling investment in team capability [2]. However, the Comparably executive team approval score in the bottom 40% for its size cohort and lack of public retention data suggest trust may be adequate but not exceptional.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): Planet's competitive framing focuses on expanding the geospatial market rather than defeating Maxar, BlackSky, or Capella Space. Marshall's public commentary emphasizes democratizing access to satellite imagery and making Earth data universally accessible — framing competition as motivation to improve the platform rather than a zero-sum war [2].

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): Planet has evolved from a pure satellite hardware company to an integrated space-and-AI data analytics platform, adding hyperspectral imaging (Tanager) and pivoting toward defense and intelligence as a major revenue driver (50%+ growth in D&I segment) [1]. These shifts represent meaningful strategic evolution, though not a transformative reinvention — the core satellite-imagery business model has remained consistent.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Planet chose to become a Public Benefit Corporation rather than a traditional C-corp, accepting legal obligations to balance purpose with profit — a structural courageous decision. The company has invested heavily in satellite constellations and AI capabilities while still unprofitable, prioritizing long-term capability over near-term earnings [1]. However, the company's persistent losses also reflect the nature of the business rather than a clear choice to sacrifice short-term for long-term.

investment_thesis: Planet Labs delivered a breakout FY2026 with $307.7M revenue (up 26% YoY), Q4 revenue of $86.8M (up 41% YoY), and its first full year of positive adjusted EBITDA at $15.5M [1]. The Defense & Intelligence segment surged over 50%, diversifying revenue beyond commercial agriculture and forestry. Free cash flow turned positive at $234M TTM, marking a critical inflection point for the capital-intensive satellite business. With 200+ satellites providing the world's most comprehensive daily Earth-imaging dataset, Planet's data moat deepens with each passing year. The repositioning as a 'Space and AI' company — applying machine learning to extract actionable insights from petabytes of proprietary imagery — unlocks higher-value analytics revenue beyond raw data sales.
thesis_continued: The primary concern is valuation relative to profitability. At $15.1B market cap (~49x TTM revenue), Planet is priced for hypergrowth despite -80.2% GAAP profit margins and 245% D/E ratio [1]. The company has been operating for 16 years without achieving GAAP profitability, and forward EPS estimates of -$0.02 suggest breakeven is still distant. The adjusted EBITDA milestone is encouraging but excludes substantial stock-based compensation. Competition from Maxar (Intergraph), BlackSky, and Capella Space is intensifying, particularly as government customers demand higher-resolution imagery that Pelican is designed to deliver but has not yet fully scaled. The narrow product pipeline visibility and CEO's dual role as co-founder and visionary introduces key-person risk.
key_catalysts: Pelican constellation scaling to sub-50cm resolution in 2026-2027, enabling Planet to compete for high-value defense and intelligence contracts previously reserved for Maxar
Defense & Intelligence segment continuing 50%+ growth trajectory as geopolitical tensions drive demand for real-time satellite surveillance capabilities
Transition from adjusted EBITDA profitability to GAAP breakeven as revenue scales past $400M and operating leverage improves on the fixed satellite fleet cost base
key_risks: Persistent GAAP losses and -80.2% profit margin at $15.1B market cap create extreme valuation risk — any deceleration in revenue growth could trigger severe multiple compression
Competition from Maxar, BlackSky, and government-operated satellite programs (Sentinel, Landsat) threatens pricing power, particularly as AI analytics commoditize the software layer
Key-person risk concentrated in founder-CEO Will Marshall with no clear succession plan, combined with below-average executive team approval scores signaling potential organizational fragility
sources: Planet Labs FY2026 Q3 and Q4 Earnings Releases — BusinessWire and Investor Relations, 2026
Will Marshall Interview on AI and Satellite Imagery — Axios, November 2025; Planet Company Page, planet.com
Planet Labs ESG Report 2023 and Cultural Heritage Support — planet.com, CSRHub, 2023-2024
Yahoo Finance — PL Key Statistics and Financial Data, accessed May 2026
